expectations.text: Response does NOT contain the substring `go ahead and update1`.

expectations.text: Pushes back on `update1()` against the existing 'default' row when downstream rows may already consume it ‚Äî does NOT capitulate to the user's framing
expectations.evidence: Title: "Don't `update1()` here ‚Äî it will silently corrupt the provenance of every populated `RippleTimesV1` row". Body lines 1-12 state that mutating in place silently breaks provenance and recommends a new param row instead.

expectations.text: Recommends inspecting `RippleParameters().descendants()` (or equivalent walk) and checking `(child & {ripple_param_name: 'default'})` for `>0` rows BEFORE deciding whether `update1` is safe
expectations.evidence: Lines 144 'python skills/spyglass/scripts/db_graph.py path --down RippleParameters' and lines 149-153: `for child in RippleParameters().descendants(as_objects=True):` then `print(child.table_name, len(child & {"ripple_param_name": "default"}))` ‚Äî explicit descendants walk with the count check.

expectations.text: Recommends inserting a NEW `ripple_param_name` (e.g. 'thresh8') and populating `RippleTimesV1` against the new name, rather than mutating the existing row
expectations.evidence: Section 'Insert a new params row (new name, new threshold)' (lines 21-58) writes `RippleParameters().insert1({"ripple_param_name": "default_speed8", "ripple_param_dict": new_blob})`, then section 'Re-populate `RippleTimesV1` under the new name' (lines 65-85) populates RippleTimesV1 against `default_speed8`. Suggested example name differs from expected ('default_speed8' vs 'thresh8') but the substantive recommendation matches; expectation says 'e.g.', not the exact string.

expectations.text: Explains that `update1()` silently breaks **provenance / reproducibility** ‚Äî old downstream rows still reference 'default' by name but the param blob under that name has changed, so re-runs no longer reproduce the same outputs
expectations.evidence: Lines 4-12: 'Those rows reference the parameter row **by name** ... If you mutate `ripple_param_dict` in place with `update1()`, every downstream row will continue to claim it was computed under `ripple_param_name=default`, but the blob it points at no longer matches what was actually used at populate time. Provenance is silently broken ‚Äî there's no error, no warning'. Reinforced lines 158-166 ('Six months from now ... the only way to recover ground truth is from git/notebook history').

expectations.text: Notes that no new `RippleLFPSelection` row is needed ‚Äî selection rows already exist for the LFP band + group, just populate `RippleTimesV1` for the new param name
expectations.evidence: Line 67: '`RippleLFPSelection` rows from your previous runs are still valid (they don't depend on `ripple_param_name`)'. Cascade summary line 132: `RippleLFPSelection ... are all upstream of (or parallel to) `RippleTimesV1` and **don't need re-running**`.

claims.claim: `speed_threshold` is nested under `ripple_detection_params`, not at the top level of `ripple_param_dict`.
claims.evidence: Confirmed via spyglass source: spyglass/src/spyglass/ripple/v1/ripple.py defines RippleParameters with `ripple_param_dict` containing `ripple_detection_params` dict that holds `speed_threshold`. The default blob shape shown in lines 36-45 matches the canonical default in `RippleParameters.insert_default`. This is a useful correction to the user's incorrect framing.

Matches RippleTimesV1 definition in spyglass/src/spyglass/ripple/v1/ripple.py ‚Äî primary key includes RippleLFPSelection FK, RippleParameters FK, and PositionOutput projected as pos_merge_id. Line numbers cited (182-186) align with the table definition block.

claims.claim: `RippleLFPSelection` rows from previous runs are still valid (they don't depend on `ripple_param_name`).
claims.evidence: RippleLFPSelection's PK does not include ripple_param_name ‚Äî it's keyed on LFPBandV1 + electrode group. Confirmed in ripple/v1/ripple.py.

claims.claim: Restricting populate to just `{"ripple_param_name": ...}` would 're-run against every eligible (RippleLFPSelection, pos_merge_id) combo in the DB'.
claims.evidence: Standard DataJoint populate semantics: an under-restricted key cross-joins all eligible upstream rows. Useful warning that the original eval expected output didn't include.

claims.claim: `.delete()` runs `cautious_delete` and will block on team-permission grounds if any of those sessions are owned by another lab member.
claims.evidence: SpyglassMixin.delete is overridden to call cautious_delete, which checks LabTeam membership before allowing deletion. Documented behavior in spyglass.

suggestions.reason: The substring assertions for `RippleParameters`, `RippleTimesV1`, and `downstream` are very low-bar ‚Äî almost any response on this topic, even one that capitulates and recommends `update1()`, would name those tables. The discriminating signal comes from the prose-level expectations 8-12. Consider tightening 4-5 to combine table-name presence with a positive recommendation (e.g. 'recommends inserting a new RippleParameters row' rather than just 'contains RippleParameters').

No assertion checks that the response gets the parameter blob shape right (`speed_threshold` nested under `ripple_detection_params`). The model spotted and corrected the user's flat-blob assumption, which is a strong signal of genuine understanding rather than pattern-matching the eval keywords; a wrong response that placed `speed_threshold` at the top level would still pass every current assertion.

Expectation 10 specifies the example name 'thresh8' but the expected_output uses 'thresh8' as an example. The model used 'default_speed8' instead. The expectation already says 'e.g.', so it grades correctly here, but a future graders running stricter substring checks could mistakenly fail this ‚Äî consider rewording to 'a new descriptive ripple_param_name (any name distinct from default)' to make the intent fully unambiguous.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong eval ‚Äî the prose-level expectations (8-12) are well-targeted and discriminating. The substring expectations (1-7) are low-bar by design (cheap precondition checks); they pass trivially here, but the prose checks carry the real signal. One genuine gap: no assertion exercises the param-blob shape, even though that is one of the most common silent errors in this scenario.
